exemplar.title: To what extent do different light intensities with different colors/wavelengths affect a light-dependent resistorâs resistance (LDR) in an electric circuit?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent do different light intensities with different colors/wavelengths affect a light-dependent resistorâs resistance (LDR) in an electric circuit?

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Raw and processed data tables include clear headings, units and instrument uncertainties
Graphs are neat, correctly labelled, precise and include error bars
Uncertainties are propagated to resistance values and displayed consistently
Appropriate statistical treatment (standard deviation, standard error) and clear interpretation of non-Ohmic behaviour
weaknesses: Distance uncertainty (Â±0.5 cm) was not propagated into intensity calculations
Inconsistent significant-figure usage in processed results
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data recording, processing and presentation are both clear and precise, satisfying all the descriptors for the top band. Instrumental uncertainties are treated appropriately and consistently, and the quality of processing is high despite a few minor formatting and propagation omissions. Awarding the maximum score of 6 reflects the rigor and precision of the analysis.

strengths: Research question embedded in a highly specific real-world context (camera auto-exposure)
Comprehensive theoretical background linking Ohmâs law, photoelectric effect and intensity laws
Detailed apparatus list and procedure allowing reproduction with few ambiguities
Clear, testable hypothesis predicting non-linear resistance changes
weaknesses: Number of repeats and exact geometry of lux-meter are not fully specified
Spectral sensitivity data for filters and justification of inverse-square law application are missing
Key methodological factors (e.g. Ît in power calculation) are introduced but not controlled or explained in depth
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates exceptional understanding of context and theory, and provides a procedure that another investigator could reproduce with minimal ambiguity. Although some methodological details (number of trials, filter spectra, time control) are under-explained, overall the design sits firmly in the 5-6 band. A score of 5 recognizes the strong contextualisation and mostly complete methodology while acknowledging a few remaining omissions.

strengths: Conclusion is directly supported by the two key graphs and RÂ² values
Findings are linked to the theory of photo-excited carriers and expected exponential behavior
Clear statement of non-Ohmic nature of the LDR and relevance to wider scientific context
weaknesses: Quantitative fit parameters (e.g. slope, exact RÂ²) are not cited
Literature values or numerical comparisons for decay exponent are omitted
Citations of external studies remain generic rather than specific
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is well justified and fully consistent with the analysis, providing scientific context through theory and graphical evidence. While inclusion of explicit numerical fit parameters and literature comparisons would strengthen the justification, the overall coherence and relevance to accepted scientific models justify a high score of 5 in this criterion.

strengths: Specific methodological weaknesses are identified (e.g. ambient light, meter internal resistance)
Realistic and relevant improvements are proposed (e.g. thermostat control, true ohmmeter)
Limitations table shows good structure and direct linkage to procedural steps
weaknesses: Quantitative assessment of the impact of each limitation is not provided
Some suggested improvements lack explanation of how they reduce specific uncertainties
One limitation entry lacks a corresponding mitigation action
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation describes specific strengths and limitations and proposes realistic improvements, meeting the 3-4 band descriptors. The impact of each weakness could be analyzed more quantitatively, and some improvements lack full explanation of their uncertainty-reducing effects. A score of 4 appropriately reflects the depth and relevance of the evaluation while recognizing room for more detailed analysis.

strandSelections.reasoning: The student states a focused question (âTo what extent do different light intensities with different colours/wavelengths affect an LDRâs resistance â¦ ?â) and embeds it in a clearly-defined context of camera auto-exposure and LDR behaviour.  Background theory (Ohmâs law, photoelectric effect, intensityâdistance laws) and real-world motivation are provided, so the reader understands exactly why and how the investigation is being pursued.  The context is therefore specific and appropriate rather than merely broad.

Instrumental uncertainties are quoted, standard deviations and standard errors are calculated, propagated to resistance, and displayed as error bars on graphs.  Although distance uncertainty is not propagated and some resistance errors seem underestimated, overall treatment is appropriate and consistent.

Methodological considerations are identified (temperature, room light, instrument resolution, distance, voltage supply) and some controls are described, yet they are only stated/described rather than fully explained.  For example, the influence of lux-meter precision on resistance uncertainty or the rationale for 50 cm sourceâdetector spacing is not explored.  This corresponds to the middle of the 3-4 band.

Specific weaknesses are described in a table (ambient light, temperature, meter internal resistance, indirect resistance measurement).  The impact is mentioned qualitatively (âaffect readingâ, âhigh error barsâ) but not analysed quantitatively.  This meets the descriptor for âdescribes specific methodological weaknessesâ.

Apparatus lists, variable tables and a ten-step procedure allow another student to replicate the work in principle; however, important details are missing or ambiguous: the number of repeats is only implied (two trials), the exact lux-meter geometry is not fixed, and colour-filter spectral data are absent.  Thus reproduction would be possible but with âfew ambiguitiesâ, aligning with the 3-4 band, top of range.

Averages, standard deviations, propagated uncertainties and resistances are calculated correctly and linear/exponential regressions are attempted.  However, the regression method is superficially explained (incorrect SS formula) and only two trials reduce statistical reliability.  These moderate omissions place the work in the 3-4 band, upper end.

Realistic improvementsâuse of thermostat, true ohmmeter, darker environmentâare suggested and briefly elaborated, but the explanation of how each would enhance validity/precision is limited.  Hence âdescribedâ rather than âexplainedâ, placing it in the 3-4 band with a score of 3.

Raw-data tables include headings, units and instrument uncertainties; processed tables and graphs are neat with correctly-labelled axes and error bars, so communication is both clear and largely precise.  Minor formatting slips (mixed bold, superscripts) prevent a flawless impression, hence 5 rather than 6.

The student links findings to theory of photo-excited carriers and expected exponential behaviour, and compares Ohmic versus non-Ohmic responses, but citations are generic and no numerical literature values (e.g., typical Î³ in RâI^-Î³) are consulted.  Comparison is ârelevantâ though not thoroughly justified, fitting low end of 3-4 band.

The conclusion restates that resistance decreases exponentially with lux and that the LDR is non-ohmic, directly supported by the two presented graphs and RÂ² value.  Quantitative reference remains limited (no gradient comparison, no explicit functional fit parameters), preventing a fully âjustifiedâ rating, so 3-4 band is appropriate.

annotations.comment: Equation $$R=\frac{V}{I}$$ is introduced without discussing its limitations when applied to non-Ohmic devices, which is central to this investigation.
The list of factors affecting resistance lacks explicit connection to the LDR used; include spectral sensitivity data of the blue and red filters.
The inverse-square law (Equation $$I = \frac{P}{4\pi d^2}$$) is derived but not justified over the simpler $$I=\frac{P}{A}$$ approach; clarify its relevance to the experimental setup.
The discussion of Maxwell versus Einstein lacks critical comparison; explicitly state how the particle nature of light influences LDR response.
Equations for photon energy are well presented but lack direct linkage to the lux measurement and resulting resistance changes; clarify this connection.
The research question is precisely stated and embedded within a specific real-world context, demonstrating clear motivation and depth of understanding.
The introductionâs purpose statement is too general; it would benefit from explicitly linking methodological steps to the research question and variables.
A clear and testable hypothesis is provided, correctly predicting a non-linear decrease in LDR resistance with increasing light intensity.
The conversion from luminous flux to lux assumes a constant efficacy \(\eta\); discuss the validity of this approximation for your bulb types.
The exposition of the electromagnetic spectrum effectively links color to wavelength, providing clear theoretical context.
The mechanism of LDR operation is well explained, using photon-carrier arguments that set up the expected exponential behavior.
Procedural steps omit the explicit number of repeats; state and justify the number of trials for statistical reliability.
The independent-variable table lists âcircuit arrangementâ without a measurement method; include details or remove this row.
Procedural descriptions for filter changes are ambiguous; specify the order and exact positioning for each bulb power level.
Raw data tables include uncertainties for each instrument and are clearly labeled, supporting reproducibility.
Uncertainty propagation omits the Â±0.5 cm distance error, which contributes to power/intensity uncertainty; include this term.
Processed data tables show inconsistent significant-figure usage (e.g., 11.96 vs 11.828); apply a uniform rounding policy.
The currentâvoltage graph is neatly presented with error bars, facilitating precise interpretation of Ohmic vs non-Ohmic behavior.
The description âLDRs are not non-ohmicâ confuses with a double negative; revise to âLDRs are non-Ohmicâ for clarity.
The regression SS formulas use addition signs instead of differences; correct to \(SS_{res}=\sum(I_{actual}-I_{pred})^2\) etc.
The time parameter \(\Delta t\) in the power equation is not defined or controlled in the methodology; specify measurement duration.
The exponential decay in the lux vs resistance plot is very clear, strongly supporting the hypothesized relationship.
The first row in the limitations table lacks an associated improvement; include a concrete action to mitigate room-lighting effects.
Suggested improvements are realistic but lack explanation of how they will quantitatively reduce uncertainties; add justifications.
The conclusion restates findings but omits quantitative fit parameters (e.g., slope, RÂ²); include these to fully justify claims.

rubricData.name: Physics IA
rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â
